patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, 22, a vague, evasive night-shift warehouse worker. Start with: “I’ve been wiped out for months, and my belly feels kind of full and sore on the left side.” Volunteer only: constant fatigue despite sleep, getting winded on stairs, early fullness, left-under-ribs pressure, occasional yellow eyes, looking pale.

Be reluctant with details; answer briefly and minimize severity. If asked directly, mention: past “enlarged spleen,” possibly enlarged liver, dizziness on standing, racing heart with walking, intermittent dark urine (worse when dehydrated), easy bruising, right-upper-belly pain after greasy meals and “maybe gallstones,” headaches when exhausted, more colds.

Do NOT mention unless specifically probed: craving/chewing ice, restless legs, heavier periods, toe/foot flares, tingling hands, unintentional weight loss.

Resist admitting: drinking more alcohol to cope, skipping labs/follow-ups due to fear and cost, avoiding exercise from embarrassment.

Bring up misconceptions: worry it’s a lung blood clot because of cough/chest tightness; prefer OTC supplements; assume genetic testing/specialists are for rich people; fear bills/collections and losing your job if referred.

If clinician is empathetic, soften slightly and admit fear and finances, but still hedge.

Hypersplenism causing thrombocytopenia, complicating interpretation of cytopenias

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital dyserythropoietic anemia (CDA)
required_evidence: Chronic anemia with inappropriately low or only mildly elevated reticulocyte count (ineffective erythropoiesis)
Hepatosplenomegaly consistent with extramedullary hematopoiesis and/or hemolysis
CBC pattern compatible with CDA (often macrocytosis or normocytosis with elevated RDW)
Exclusion of alternative causes of hepatosplenomegaly/anemia (e.g., acute blood loss, nutritional deficiency, autoimmune hemolysis) based on history and available labs
Clinical reasoning that pulmonary and rheumatologic differentials (CTEPH, COP, AIP, RA) do not explain isolated hepatosplenomegaly with anemia pattern

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Decreased (chronic anemia; severity variable, often mild-to-moderate)
expected_lab_results.RBC count: Decreased or inappropriately normal for degree of anemia
expected_lab_results.MCV: Normal to increased (often macrocytosis, depending on CDA subtype)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Normal to mildly increased but inappropriately low for degree of anemia (reflecting ineffective erythropoiesis)
expected_lab_results.Absolute reticulocyte count: Normal to mildly increased but inappropriately low for degree of anemia

medications: Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Packed red blood cell transfusion 10-15 mL/kg IV per transfusion session PRN symptomatic anemia or hemoglobin below individualized threshold
non_pharmacological: Refer to hematology for definitive subtype workup (peripheral smear review, hemolysis/iron studies, bone marrow evaluation and/or genetic testing as indicated)
Avoid empiric iron supplementation unless iron deficiency is confirmed; monitor for iron overload if transfused or if ferritin elevated
Vaccination review and update (especially if splenectomy is ever considered): pneumococcal, meningococcal, Hib, annual influenza
Counsel on recognizing anemia symptoms (fatigue, dyspnea, palpitations) and when to seek urgent care
Consider curative evaluation for hematopoietic stem cell transplantation in severe transfusion-dependent disease (specialist-directed)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 2-4 weeks; earlier (within 48-72 hours) if transfused or if symptoms worsen

must_include: CDA is a rare inherited disorder where the bone marrow makes red cells inefficiently (ineffective erythropoiesis)
This can cause chronic anemia and enlargement of the spleen and liver
Why reticulocytes may not rise appropriately despite anemia
Need for specialist testing to confirm subtype and guide long-term management
Folic acid supports red blood cell production and is commonly used in chronic hemolytic/ineffective erythropoiesis states
Transfusions are supportive and based on symptoms and hemoglobin level, not a fixed number alone
If transfusions become frequent, iron overload monitoring and treatment may be needed
Potential future options (specialist-led) including splenectomy in select cases and stem cell transplant for severe disease
Discuss expected course (chronic condition) and goals: symptom control, avoid complications (iron overload, gallstones, thrombosis in some contexts)
Review penicillin allergy and reassure it will be documented to avoid reactions
Safety-net symptoms requiring urgent evaluation (syncope, chest pain, severe dyspnea, rapidly worsening fatigue, signs of infection)
Avoid self-starting iron supplements unless advised after iron studies
Maintain routine follow-up and lab monitoring as recommended by hematology
If splenomegaly is significant: avoid contact sports/trauma risk and report left upper quadrant pain promptly

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms (fatigue, dyspnea, jaundice), bleeding history, infection history, transfusion history, family history/consanguinity, medication/supplement use (especially iron), pregnancy status if relevant, and red flags (syncope, chest pain).

Order initial anemia/hemolysis workup and CDA-relevant evaluation (CBC with indices, reticulocyte count, smear, bilirubin/LDH/haptoglobin, iron studies, LFTs, B12/folate, DAT, ferritin; consider viral testing if indicated).
Interpret results to support ineffective erythropoiesis/hemolysis pattern and assess severity; note inappropriately low/normal reticulocytes for degree of anemia and exclude common acquired causes.
State likely congenital dyserythropoietic anemia vs differential; indicate need for specialist confirmation (bone marrow morphology, genetic testing) and subtype-guided management.

Explain diagnosis in patient-centered language including ineffective erythropoiesis, chronic anemia, splenohepatomegaly, and why reticulocytes may not rise appropriately; set expectations and discuss confirmatory testing.
Critical safety check before any prescribing: confirm and document penicillin allergy details (reaction type/severity, timing) and other allergies.
Non-critical safety step before prescribing: review current medications/supplements for interactions (e.g., folate, transfusion-related meds, chelators if considered later).
Initiate appropriate supportive therapy (e.g., folic acid) and avoid empiric iron unless iron deficiency proven; ensure allergy and interaction checks completed first.

If transfusion history or suspected overload: order baseline iron overload monitoring (ferritin, transferrin saturation) and plan periodic monitoring; consider imaging referral via hematology if needed.

Explain treatment plan: folic acid rationale, transfusion strategy based on symptoms/Hb, iron overload risk and monitoring/chelation if frequent transfusions, and specialist-led options (splenectomy select cases, stem cell transplant for severe disease).

Address concerns and safety-net: chronic course, goals (symptom control, prevent iron overload/gallstones/thrombosis in some contexts), document penicillin allergy, urgent symptoms (syncope, chest pain, severe dyspnea, rapidly worsening fatigue, infection signs).
Lifestyle counseling: avoid self-starting iron supplements; adhere to hematology follow-up/lab monitoring; if splenomegaly significant avoid contact sports/trauma and report LUQ pain promptly.
Arrange hematology referral/ongoing follow-up for confirmatory testing, monitoring (Hb, hemolysis markers, iron overload), and long-term management planning.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, providing return precautions, and documenting plan.

general_concerns: Believes hereditary spastic paraplegia usually turns into ALS or multiple sclerosis if it keeps getting worse.
Thinks that using a cane or ankle-foot brace will make her legs 'weaker' and accelerate disability, so she tries to avoid mobility aids.
Assumes that if her MRI was 'normal' in the past, the diagnosis must be wrong and the symptoms are likely due to stress or poor posture.
Believes that intense strength training and pushing through spasticity will 'break up the stiffness' and stop progression, even if it increases spasms and fatigue.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old anxious but clear-speaking woman. Start by saying: “My legs have been getting stiffer and I keep tripping when I walk.” Volunteer: legs feel tight/stiff when you first start walking, feet catch on the ground, stairs are harder, you’re slower and your gait looks awkward. If asked, add: gradual worsening over years, night calf cramps/jumpy legs, fatigue when standing, urinary urgency, a couple falls, and a family member had “walking problems.” Do not mention unless specifically asked: uneven shoe wear from dragging toes, holding walls/furniture, avoiding long walks/uneven ground, worse with stress/cold, constipation. Only disclose if directly asked with reassurance: occasional urinary accidents, less satisfying sex, feeling down/irritable from fear of losing independence. Express misconceptions: worry about MS; wonder about Charcot-Marie-Tooth due to tingling and high arches; recall childhood clumsiness. Be hesitant about PT copays, workplace accommodations, and device/home-mod coverage. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit fear, and ask what this could be and what help is realistic.

Sertraline 50 mg once daily
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU once daily
confounders: Borderline vitamin B12 status with elevated methylmalonic acid (can contribute to gait disturbance/neuropathy-like symptoms)
History of recurrent mechanical low back pain (may mimic radiculopathy-related gait issues)
SSRI use (sertraline) may contribute to fatigue and subjective weakness

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hereditary spastic paraplegia (HSP)
required_evidence: Progressive, predominantly lower-extremity spasticity with pyramidal signs (hyperreflexia, clonus, extensor plantar responses)
Spastic gait with leg stiffness/scissoring; possible urinary urgency
Relative preservation of strength early; sensory exam often normal or mild vibratory loss depending on subtype
Family history consistent with inherited neurodegenerative disorder (autosomal dominant/recessive/X-linked) OR early onset with chronic progression
Exclusion of structural/inflammatory/metabolic mimics (e.g., normal/basic labs; neuroimaging if available without alternative explanation)
If available: genetic testing identifying a pathogenic/likely pathogenic variant in an HSP-associated gene

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no anemia expected in isolated HSP)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Creatini: Typically normal renal function expected unless comorbidity

medications: Baclofen 5 mg PO three times daily; titrate every 3-7 days as tolerated to 10-20 mg PO three times daily (max individualized; avoid abrupt withdrawal)
Tizanidine 2 mg PO at bedtime; may increase by 2-4 mg every 3-7 days to 2-4 mg PO three times daily as needed/tolerated (monitor sedation and hypotension)
non_pharmacological: Referral to neurology (movement disorders/neuromuscular) for confirmation, phenotyping, and genetic testing/counseling
Physical therapy focusing on stretching, strengthening, gait training, and spasticity management; home stretching program
Occupational therapy for mobility aids and activities of daily living optimization
Assistive devices/orthotics as indicated (e.g., ankle-foot orthoses, cane/walker)
Spasticity triggers review and management (pain, constipation, infections, poor sleep); bowel regimen if constipation contributes
Consider botulinum toxin injections for focal spasticity if inadequate response/intolerance to oral agents
Fall-risk assessment and home safety evaluation
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks for spasticity control, function, adverse effects, and dose titration; earlier (1-2 weeks) if significant sedation, weakness, or falls.

must_include: HSP is a group of inherited disorders causing progressive stiffness/spasticity mainly in the legs due to corticospinal tract involvement
Course is usually slowly progressive; severity and associated features vary by subtype
Diagnosis is clinical plus exclusion of mimics; genetic testing can confirm and guide family counseling
No curative therapy for most HSP; management focuses on symptom control, mobility, and preventing complications
Spasticity medications (baclofen/tizanidine) are started low and titrated; common side effects include drowsiness, dizziness, weakness; avoid abrupt baclofen discontinuation
Therapy/orthotics and assistive devices are key to maintaining function; options exist if oral meds are insufficient (botulinum toxin, intrathecal baclofen evaluation)
Discuss driving/work/fall risk and strategies to reduce falls
Discuss inheritance patterns and offer referral for genetic counseling and family planning implications

Red flags requiring prompt reassessment: rapidly worsening symptoms, new sensory level, severe back pain, acute bladder retention, or new seizures/cognitive regression suggesting alternative diagnosis
Daily stretching and regular low-impact exercise as tolerated; avoid prolonged immobility
Sleep hygiene and management of triggers (constipation, infections) that can worsen spasticity
Home safety modifications (remove trip hazards, appropriate footwear) and use of mobility aids when recommended

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history to confirm spastic paraparesis pattern, progression, functional impact, family history, and screen for mimics/red flags (sensory level, acute bladder retention, severe back pain, rapid decline, seizures/cognitive regression).
Clarify current meds/substances and comorbidities relevant to spasticity treatment (sedation risk, liver disease, hypotension, renal function), plus pregnancy plans and goals of care.

Order basic evaluation to exclude common mimics/secondary causes as appropriate to context (e.g., B12, MMA if needed, TSH, copper/ceruloplasmin if indicated, HIV/HTLV-1 if risk, syphilis testing, CMP/CBC; consider MRI brain/spine referral if not previously done or red flags).
Review results to ensure no alternative diagnosis is suggested and that prescribing is safe (e.g., hepatic/renal function if considering tizanidine/baclofen).
State working diagnosis of hereditary spastic paraplegia (or probable HSP) after clinical assessment and exclusion of mimics; note need for genetic confirmation and counseling.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, expected course/variability, role of genetic testing; address patient concerns, red flags; provide lifestyle and safety counseling.
Critical safety check before prescribing any medication for spasticity.
Check for drug-drug interactions (non-critical but expected) with current meds/substances before prescribing baclofen/tizanidine or alternatives.

Initiate symptom treatment plan (e.g., baclofen or tizanidine) with low starting dose and titration, counsel on side effects and avoiding abrupt baclofen discontinuation; consider non-pharm referrals (PT/OT, orthotics).
Arrange follow-up to assess spasticity response, adverse effects, falls, function, and need for escalation (botulinum toxin, intrathecal baclofen evaluation) and to coordinate genetic counseling.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions/red flags, and follow-up plan.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Pituitary region epidermoid or dermoid cyst (sellar/suprasellar inclusion cyst) causing mass effect with possible hypopituitarism
required_evidence: Sellar/suprasellar cystic lesion on MRI/CT consistent with inclusion cyst (epidermoid: diffusion restriction; dermoid: fat signal with possible chemical meningitis if ruptured)
Symptoms consistent with sellar mass effect (e.g., headache, visual field deficits) and/or pituitary hormone abnormalities

Pituitary lab evaluation showing possible central endocrine deficits (e.g., low AM cortisol with low/inappropriately normal ACTH; low free T4 with low/inappropriately normal TSH) and/or stalk-effect hyperprolactinemia
Exclusion of primary thyroid/adrenal failure patterns on available labs (i.e., not high ACTH with low cortisol; not high TSH with low free T4)

expected_lab_results.Sodium: May be low if secondary adrenal insufficiency or SIADH; may be high if central diabetes insipidus (less common with pure pituitary lesions but possible with suprasellar involvement)
expected_lab_results.Serum osmolality: Low in hyponatremia/SIADH; high in hypernatremia/diabetes insipidus
expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality: Inappropriately high in SIADH (concentrated urine despite low serum osmolality); inappropriately low in diabetes insipidus (dilute urine despite high serum osmolality)
expected_lab_results.Morning serum cortisol (8 AM): Low if secondary adrenal insufficiency due to hypopituitarism; normal if axis preserved
expected_lab_results.ACTH: Low or inappropriately normal with low cortisol in secondary adrenal insufficiency
expected_lab_results.TSH: Low or inappropriately normal in central hypothyroidism (do not expect appropriately elevated TSH)
expected_lab_results.Prolactin: Mild to moderate elevation consistent with stalk effect (typically <200 ng/mL); can be normal

medications: Hydrocortisone 15-25 mg PO daily in divided doses (e.g., 15 mg AM, 5 mg PM) if secondary adrenal insufficiency suspected/confirmed
Hydrocortisone 50-100 mg IV q6-8h if adrenal crisis suspected or perioperative stress dosing
Levothyroxine 1.6 mcg/kg/day PO daily (start only after glucocorticoid replacement if adrenal insufficiency possible)
Desmopressin 0.05-0.1 mg PO q12h (or 10-20 mcg intranasal daily) if central diabetes insipidus confirmed
non_pharmacological: Urgent neurosurgery referral for definitive management; surgical resection/decompression is standard for symptomatic dermoid/epidermoid cysts or those causing endocrine/visual compromise
Ophthalmology evaluation with formal visual field testing if any visual symptoms or suprasellar extension
Endocrinology consultation for comprehensive pituitary axis assessment and perioperative hormone management
If hyponatremia present: manage based on etiology (fluid restriction for SIADH; treat adrenal insufficiency if present); avoid rapid sodium correction
Patient education on adrenal insufficiency sick-day rules and medical alert identification if on steroids
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks for endocrine review and symptom reassessment; sooner (same day/24-48h) if visual deficits, severe headache, significant sodium abnormality, or adrenal insufficiency suspected; postoperative endocrine follow-up at 2-6 weeks and then periodic monitoring

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 22-year-old woman. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “I can’t stop feeling hungry all the time, and I keep gaining weight no matter what we try.” Volunteer: constant hunger even right after eating, rapid weight gain, getting very upset/meltdown when told you can’t have food, and daytime tiredness.

If asked, share: you sometimes sneak food/eat in secret; lifelong low muscle tone/weakness; learning difficulties needing extra help; shorter than many family members; loud snoring and sometimes seeming to stop breathing; frequent constipation; skin picking.

Do not mention unless directly asked: you don’t feel full, don’t sweat much, low pain sensitivity, temperature feels “off,” low sexual interest and delayed puberty.

Be reluctant/embarrassed and only admit if asked gently: taking food from others/trash, and occasional bedwetting/accidents when constipated.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s thyroid or depression. Raise cost/insurance fears about genetic testing/specialists; think nutrition/exercise programs are “luxury”; fear job accommodations could cut hours. If clinician is empathetic, you open up more and feel relieved.

Genetic testing - PWS methylation analys.value: Abnormal: absence of paternal 15q11-q13 expression pattern

Genetic testing - DNA sequencing (SNORD1.value: Pathogenic point mutation detected (paternally inherited/de novo)

Chromosomal microarray (15q11-q13).normal_range: No clinically significant copy number variants

medications: Levothyroxine 50 mcg PO daily
Metformin ER 500 mg PO twice daily with meals
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO daily
Topiramate 50 mg PO nightly
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg PO daily
comorbidities: Severe obesity (hyperphagia associated with Prader-Willi syndrome)
Obstructive sleep apnea (history, on CPAP at night)
Mild intellectual disability with behavioral dysregulation (skin picking, compulsivity)
confounders: Obstructive sleep apnea and obesity hypoventilation risk can mimic fatigue and cognitive slowing from hypothyroidism
Topiramate-associated appetite suppression/weight changes may confound assessment of hyperphagia severity
Oral contraceptive use can obscure evaluation of endogenous gonadotropin/estradiol status and menstrual pattern
Behavioral dysregulation and skin picking can be mistaken for primary psychiatric illness
NAFLD-related transaminitis may be misattributed to medication effects without imaging correlation

Clinical context consistent with PWS-related hyperphagia/obesity risk and endocrine/metabolic monitoring needs

expected_lab_results.Fasting plasma glucose: Often normal to mildly elevated; may be in prediabetes range (100–125 mg/dL) or diabetes (≥126 mg/dL) depending on severity
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: May be normal to elevated; prediabetes 5.7–6.4% or diabetes ≥6.5% if significant dysglycemia
expected_lab_results.Fasting insulin: Often elevated, consistent with insulin resistance (hyperinsulinemia)
expected_lab_results.Triglycerides: Often elevated; may be markedly elevated with obesity/insulin resistance
expected_lab_results.ALT: May be mildly elevated, consistent with possible NAFLD risk in obesity/insulin resistance

medications: Metformin 500 mg PO once daily with food, increase to 500 mg PO twice daily after 1-2 weeks as tolerated (if prediabetes/diabetes or significant insulin resistance and no contraindications)
If LDL remains elevated after lifestyle therapy or if diabetes present with elevated ASCVD risk: Atorvastatin 10-20 mg PO once daily (age/ASCVD-risk dependent; use guideline-based intensity)
non_pharmacological: Structured environmental food security (locked food access, supervised meals, consistent routines) and caregiver-led calorie control tailored to PWS
Dietary pattern emphasizing high-fiber, nutrient-dense foods; limit sugar-sweetened beverages and refined carbohydrates; portion control
Regular physical activity plan adapted to ability (goal ≥150 min/week moderate activity if feasible) plus strength training 2 days/week
Weight-management program with multidisciplinary team (endocrinology, nutrition, behavioral/psychology, physical therapy as needed)
Screen and manage comorbidities common in PWS (sleep apnea, hypothyroidism, hypogonadism, scoliosis); ensure routine endocrine follow-up
If ALT elevated: counsel on NAFLD risk and weight loss; consider further evaluation per clinician judgment
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck metabolic labs (fasting glucose/A1c, lipids, ALT) in 3 months after lifestyle initiation or medication changes; sooner (4-6 weeks) if starting/titrating metformin or statin and to assess tolerance/adherence.

PWS increases risk of hyperphagia, obesity, insulin resistance/diabetes, abnormal cholesterol, and fatty liver

Lifestyle and environmental controls are foundational in PWS (food security/supervision is medical necessity, not willpower)
If metformin is used: purpose (improve insulin resistance/glucose), gradual titration, common GI side effects, take with food
If statin is used: purpose (lower LDL and cardiovascular risk), adherence importance, report muscle pain or dark urine, periodic lab monitoring as appropriate

Review allergies: amoxicillin (urticarial rash) and latex (contact dermatitis) and how they affect care

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 24-year-old woman who is anxious and a bit vague. Start with: you’ve been getting overwhelmed at school and can’t focus or keep up, especially with reading/writing. Volunteer that you’ve always learned slower, were in special classes, get constipated a lot, and that your thumbs/fingertips look wide with big nails; people comment on your thick eyebrows and “different” smile.

If asked about childhood/development, share feeding problems as a baby, delayed sitting/walking, frequent ear infections and sometimes trouble hearing, being short, sensitivity to loud sounds/routine changes, snoring and waking tired, and brief staring spells.

Do not mention eye crossing/early glasses, crowded teeth/dental work, loose joints/clumsiness, heartburn/cough after eating, or irregular painful periods unless directly asked.

Be reluctant to admit skin picking/nail biting until bleeding, occasional bedwetting/accidents with constipation, and panic-like moments; say you’re embarrassed and don’t want to seem “crazy.”

Bring up your parent’s worries: PANDAS after a sore throat, headaches with nausea/light sensitivity suggesting a “brain problem,” and confusion about a different gene/Rubinstein-Taybi type.

Express misconceptions: insurance will deny genetic counseling/testing as “not medically necessary,” a genetic diagnosis will hurt jobs/insurance, and you must pay out-of-pocket so you avoid appointments.

If the clinician is empathic, you soften, look relieved, and share more details.

Review available genetic test report and any prior screening results (echo, audiology, ophthalmology, sleep study, EEG/brain MRI if done) to ground diagnosis and next-step referrals.

State diagnosis: Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome due to EP300 haploinsufficiency; document diagnostic basis (clinical features + genetic confirmation) and note key differentials already considered/ruled out.

Explain diagnosis in plain language, including genetic cause, typical features (e.g., broadened digits), that it is not caused by family actions, and recurrence risk depends on de novo vs inherited; recommend parental testing when appropriate.

Explain management: no curative medication; focus on surveillance and supportive therapies; outline referrals (genetics, early intervention/developmental services, hearing/vision/dental; cardiac/sleep/neurology as indicated) and individualized supports/monitoring over time.

Address concerns and safety-net: comorbidities to watch for (feeding issues, sleep-disordered breathing, recurrent infections, seizures/behavioral concerns), when to seek urgent care, and why alternative diagnoses may be considered (CREBBP-related RSTS, 16p13.3 microdeletion, polymicrogyria if neurologic symptoms, PANDAS only with abrupt characteristic onset). Offer genetic counseling and community resources.

Lifestyle and preventive care counseling: vaccinations, dental hygiene, routine vision/hearing checks, structured routines and school accommodations/IEP, early intervention participation, sleep hygiene and screening for snoring/apneas.

Arrange follow-up and coordination: genetics follow-up for inheritance/recurrence counseling, developmental pediatrics/therapies, and targeted screening appointments based on symptoms and prior results.
Safety check required before any prescribing; confirm allergies and reaction types even if no medication is planned today.
If prescribing is considered, review current meds/supplements and potential interactions; non-critical but expected when a prescription is made.

Only if a specific symptomatic issue is identified (e.g., constipation, reflux, sleep issues after evaluation); ensure allergy check (critical) and interaction/contraindication checks are completed beforehand.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, providing written/next-step plan, and documenting return precautions.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 19-year-old male. Act evasive and vague: short answers, minimize details unless directly asked, and change topics when uncomfortable.

At the start, volunteer only: “I keep getting infections over and over, and this cough and fever won’t go away,” plus recurring skin boils, occasional mouth sores/white patches, constant fatigue, and lifelong itchy eczema-like rashes.

If asked targeted questions, reveal: repeat hospitalizations for pneumonia/chest infections, frequent sinus/ear infections with thick smelly mucus, slow-healing infected cuts, unintentional weight loss, hard-to-control diabetes with low C-peptide, corpus callosum didn’t develop, parents are related, and family history of serious infections.

Do not mention unless specifically probed: exertional shortness of breath, pleuritic chest pain, dental problems, hyperflexible joints/sprains, diarrhea after antibiotics, and opportunistic fungal infections.

Resist discussing genital sores, poor hygiene/bandage avoidance, and skipping meds—admit only after reassurance.

Express misconceptions: asthma/allergies/long COVID/mold. Be cost- and job/insurance-worried; say specialists/tests feel “optional” and you ration meds. If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly but stay guarded.

Insulin lispro 6 units subcutaneously with meals (carb-adjusted)
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole DS 800/160 mg orally once daily (infection prophylaxis)
Fluconazole 200 mg orally once daily (chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis prophylaxis)
Cetirizine 10 mg orally once daily (pruritus/allergic symptoms)
Vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) 2000 IU orally once daily
comorbidities: Primary immunodeficiency (hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome, autosomal recessive)
Agenesis of the corpus callosum with neurodevelopmental delay
confounders: Atopic dermatitis with secondary bacterial colonization mimicking infection flares
Poor glycemic control increasing infection risk and inflammatory markers
Chronic antifungal use potentially suppressing culture yield for candidiasis
Agenesis of the corpus callosum with communication/cognitive limitations affecting symptom reporting

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 29-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start with: “Every few weeks I get hit with the same thing—fever, mouth sores, and I feel wiped out.” Volunteer: episodes every 3–4 weeks, sudden flu-like feeling for a few days then resolve, fevers often >100.4°F with chills, painful mouth sores making eating hard, marked fatigue/weakness; between episodes you feel normal.

If asked, add: sore throat, swollen neck glands, gum tenderness/bleeding, small skin infections (pimples/boils/infected hangnail), occasional diarrhea/stomach-bug feeling, very regular pattern.

Only reveal if asked specifically about infections/complications: needing antibiotics, cuts getting infected easily, lightheaded on standing.

Resist/avoid mentioning genital sores and avoiding kissing/sex unless asked about genital symptoms/STIs/sexual history; then admit it with embarrassment and fear.

Express misconceptions: blame stress/sleep, think it’s recurring flu/COVID or an allergy; mention high-dose vitamins/herbal boosters didn’t help.

Bring up cost/job worries; prefer labs only when sick and may downplay symptoms. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit worry about being unreliable and want answers.

Peripheral smear.value: Marked neutropenia; mild normocytic anemia; mild thrombocytopenia; no blasts seen

Urinalysis.value: Leukocyte esterase negative; nitrite negative; no pyuria

medications: Zonisamide 200 mg PO nightly
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg PO daily

confounders: Zonisamide exposure (rare drug-induced neutropenia/agranulocytosis can mimic cyclic neutropenia)
Recent viral upper respiratory infection 2 weeks ago (transient marrow suppression vs infectious trigger for febrile neutropenia)
Menorrhagia (can contribute to anemia independent of cyclic hematopoiesis)
NSAID use (ibuprofen) can mask fever and delay recognition of febrile neutropenia

correct_diagnosis.primary: Cyclic neutropenia (cyclic hematopoiesis)
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital cyclic neutropenia (ELANE-associated) pending genetic confirmation
required_evidence: Serial CBCs 2–3 times weekly for 6–8 weeks showing periodic ANC nadirs approximately every ~21 days
Recurrent episodes of neutropenia (often ANC < 0.5 x10^9/L) with recovery to near-normal between episodes
Clinical correlation with cyclic symptoms (e.g., fevers, mouth ulcers, infections) during ANC nadirs
Exclusion of secondary causes of neutropenia (drug-induced, autoimmune, nutritional deficiency, marrow failure) based on history and available labs

expected_lab_results.WBC: Cyclic leukopenia with nadirs coinciding with symptoms; normal or near-normal between cycles
expected_lab_results.Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Marked periodic drops (often <0.5 x10^9/L) with regular recurrence ~every 21 days and recovery between episodes
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Typically normal; may show mild anemia if chronic inflammation/infection is frequent
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Usually normal (helps distinguish from broader marrow failure syndromes)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Usually normal unless concurrent anemia from another cause
expected_lab_results.MCV: Usually normal (macrocytosis would suggest alternative etiologies such as B12/folate deficiency or drug effect)
expected_lab_results.RDW: Usually normal; elevated RDW would suggest mixed anemia/nutritional deficiency rather than isolated cyclic neutropenia

medications: Filgrastim (G-CSF) 2–5 mcg/kg subcutaneous once daily during anticipated nadir periods or as individualized to maintain ANC >1.0 x10^9/L
Acetaminophen 650 mg oral every 6 hours as needed for fever or pain (max 3000 mg/day unless otherwise directed)
non_pharmacological: Obtain serial CBC with differential 2–3 times weekly for 6–8 weeks to confirm periodicity and guide G-CSF titration
Fever/infection precautions: urgent evaluation for temperature ≥38.0°C (100.4°F) or signs of infection, especially during ANC nadir
Oral hygiene measures to reduce mucositis/ulcer complications; prompt dental evaluation for oral lesions
Avoid unnecessary exposure to sick contacts during predicted nadirs; reinforce hand hygiene
Hematology referral for confirmation, consideration of ELANE genetic testing, and long-term management
Review current medications for potential neutropenia contribution; reassess need for zonisamide if neutropenia temporally associated (in collaboration with prescribing clinician)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 1–2 weeks; immediate same-day evaluation for fever or suspected infection during neutropenic episodes; CBC trend review at least weekly until pattern and response to therapy are established

must_include: Blood counts—especially neutrophils—drop in a predictable cycle, increasing infection risk during low points
Why serial CBCs over several weeks are needed to confirm the cyclic pattern
Red-flag symptoms that require urgent care (fever ≥38.0°C, chills, sore throat, mouth ulcers, skin infections)
Purpose of G-CSF (to raise neutrophil counts and reduce infections) and that dosing is individualized based on ANC response
Common G-CSF adverse effects (bone pain, headache) and when to report severe symptoms
Plan for monitoring labs and adjusting therapy to avoid excessive leukocytosis
Discuss medication review including zonisamide and the need to coordinate any changes with the prescriber
Clarify that penicillin allergy should be communicated before any antibiotic is prescribed
Set expectations for long-term follow-up with hematology and potential genetic testing
Infection prevention behaviors (hand hygiene, avoiding sick contacts during nadirs)
When to seek emergency care during neutropenic periods
Importance of adherence to scheduled blood draws to map the cycle

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit infection history, cyclicity of symptoms, red flags, medication/allergy history (including penicillin), and relevant family history to frame suspicion for cyclic hematopoiesis/neutropenia and immediate risk.

Provide required communication milestones: explain diagnosis and why serial CBCs are needed; red flags; infection prevention; treatment overview including G-CSF purpose, individualized dosing, adverse effects, and monitoring to avoid excessive leukocytosis; address concerns about zonisamide coordination, penicillin allergy, and long-term follow-up.
Arrange hematology follow-up and a plan for ongoing serial CBC monitoring and therapy adjustment; ensure clear instructions for urgent evaluation during febrile neutropenia.
Before any antibiotic or other prescribing, confirm and document allergy details (penicillin reaction type/severity) as a critical safety check.
Before prescribing, review interactions with current meds (explicitly including zonisamide) and any planned agents (e.g., antibiotics, G-CSF) to reduce avoidable harm.

If treatment is indicated and within scope, prescribe/plan G-CSF with individualized dosing strategy and monitoring; if prescribing antibiotics for infection, ensure allergy/contraindication checks completed first.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Sandhoff disease (GM2 gangliosidosis, HEXB deficiency)
required_evidence: Markedly decreased total beta-hexosaminidase activity (Hex A + Hex B) in leukocytes
Low/absent Hexosaminidase A activity in leukocytes with proportionally reduced/abnormal Hex A fraction due to global Hex deficiency (pattern consistent with Sandhoff rather than Tay-Sachs)
Elevated plasma GM2 ganglioside
Clinical neurodegenerative phenotype consistent with GM2 gangliosidosis (developmental regression, hypotonia/hyperreflexia, seizures, startle, vision changes/cherry-red spot) if symptoms are elicited

expected_lab_results.Hexosaminidase A activity (leukocytes): Decreased (often low/near-absent); may appear low along with low total activity
expected_lab_results.Total beta-hexosaminidase activity (Hex: Markedly decreased/near-absent (key discriminator for Sandhoff vs Tay-Sachs)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive metabolic panel - AST: Often normal or mildly elevated (non-specific; may be mildly elevated with illness or hepatic involvement)

non_pharmacological: Confirmatory testing and counseling: order/arrange HEXB gene sequencing (or targeted variant testing if family variant known) and refer to genetics/metabolic specialist

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Genetics/metabolic specialist within 1-2 weeks; neurology within 2-4 weeks (sooner if seizures/rapid progression); ongoing multidisciplinary follow-up every 1-3 months based on symptoms

must_include: Sandhoff disease is an inherited (autosomal recessive) lysosomal storage disorder causing GM2 accumulation due to HEXB-related deficiency of beta-hexosaminidase
The enzyme pattern (low total hexosaminidase) helps distinguish Sandhoff from Tay-Sachs (where total activity is usually normal with isolated Hex A deficiency)
This condition is progressive and currently has no curative therapy; care focuses on symptom control and quality of life

tool_call_sequence.description: Confirm key history and caregiver concerns: developmental regression, seizures, startle, hypotonia/spasticity, vision/hearing changes, feeding/swallowing issues, recurrent respiratory infections/aspiration, prior newborn screen/metabolic workup, family history/consanguinity, current meds, and goals of care.

Order/confirm diagnostic testing to support Sandhoff: beta-hexosaminidase enzyme assay (total hexosaminidase and Hex A/B fractions), and plan confirmatory HEXB genetic testing; consider baseline safety labs relevant to supportive meds (e.g., CMP if starting/adjusting antiepileptics).
Retrieve enzyme assay results and any prior imaging/EEG/genetics to distinguish Sandhoff from Tay-Sachs and support diagnosis.
Make/communicate working diagnosis of Sandhoff disease based on clinical picture plus enzyme pattern (low total hexosaminidase, low Hex A and Hex B) and document need for confirmatory HEXB testing.
Explain diagnosis, prognosis, inheritance, and family implications; introduce supportive-care approach and confirmatory genetic testing plan.
Critical safety step before any prescribing: verify allergies and prior adverse drug reactions (patient and relevant caregiver-reported history).
Noncritical safety step before prescribing: check interactions with current meds (e.g., antiepileptics, sedatives) and supplements.
Initiate/adjust supportive medications as indicated (e.g., levetiracetam for seizures; rescue med plan if appropriate), with monitoring plan and clear indications.

Explain treatment/supportive plan: seizure management, feeding/swallow safety, respiratory care, comfort-focused measures; provide home safety, aspiration precautions, infection prevention, and return precautions; offer psychosocial resources.
Arrange follow-up with genetics/metabolic specialist, neurology, speech/swallow therapy, nutrition, pulmonology as needed, and palliative care; plan genetic counseling and family testing discussion.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, summarizing plan, and ensuring caregiver knows when/how to seek urgent care.

nl_assertions: Explains Sandhoff disease as an inherited (autosomal recessive) lysosomal storage disorder with GM2 accumulation due to HEXB-related deficiency of beta-hexosaminidase.

Explicitly distinguishes Sandhoff from Tay-Sachs by stating that Sandhoff typically shows low total hexosaminidase activity (with low Hex A and Hex B), whereas Tay-Sachs usually has normal total activity with isolated Hex A deficiency.
States the condition is progressive and currently has no curative therapy; focuses on symptom control and quality of life.
Recommends confirmatory genetic testing (HEXB) and explains implications for family members, including carrier testing and recurrence risk.
Elicits caregiver goals and concerns (feeding, seizures, breathing, comfort, prognosis) and responds empathetically and concretely.

Provides clear return precautions: worsening/prolonged seizures, aspiration signs (coughing/choking with feeds, increased work of breathing), dehydration/poor intake, respiratory distress, fever or recurrent infections.
Outlines supportive treatment domains: seizure management, nutrition/swallow safety, respiratory care, comfort-focused measures, and the role of palliative care.
When prescribing, explains what each medication is for (e.g., levetiracetam for seizures), how dosing will be adjusted, and what monitoring/adverse effects to watch for.
Performs an allergy check before prescribing (critical) and documents the result.
Performs drug interaction and contraindication checks before prescribing when feasible; if not performed, provides a rationale and mitigations (e.g., conservative dosing, close follow-up).
Educates on home safety for seizures and aspiration risk mitigation (positioning, supervised feeding if applicable, emergency plan).
Advises infection prevention and keeping routine care/immunizations current.
Offers psychosocial supports/resources (genetic counseling, rare disease organizations, palliative support) and coordinates follow-up with genetics/metabolic and neurology.

minimal_paths.description: Recognize Sandhoff disease, communicate diagnosis and prognosis, initiate supportive management, and ensure required safety checks before prescribing.
If no immediate medication changes are needed, still complete diagnostic explanation and follow-up planning; allergy check is only required if prescribing is not performed.

acceptable_variants.description: Orders genetic testing at the same time as enzyme testing and frames diagnosis as 'suspected/probable Sandhoff' pending confirmatory HEXB results.
Performs safety checks immediately after medication reconciliation, before discussing specific prescriptions, then proceeds to counseling and prescribing.

Includes a contraindication check step explicitly before prescribing (even though not listed as an available action) by incorporating it into the interaction check rationale and documentation within the prescribing workflow.

general_concerns: Thinks her swelling episodes are mostly an allergic reaction and that carrying an epinephrine auto-injector is the main solution.
Believes stress and certain foods are the primary cause of hereditary angioedema attacks, so she focuses on elimination diets rather than preventive planning.
Assumes her blood pressure medication (fosinopril) is unrelated to swelling and is safe to continue because she has taken it for months without immediate problems.
Thinks antihistamines and steroids should reliably stop attacks if taken early, and worries that failure to respond means the episode is 'in her head.'
Believes hereditary angioedema is contagious or could be 'caught' by close contact, so she avoids hugging students when she feels swelling starting.

Thinks seeking emergency care for swelling is financially irresponsible unless she is 'actively choking,' so she delays care to avoid ER bills.
Believes genetic testing and specialist visits are optional 'luxuries' and tries to manage only through primary care to limit out-of-pocket costs.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 28-year-old elementary school teacher. You speak clearly but sound anxious and scared. Start by saying your lips and cheeks began swelling a few hours ago “out of nowhere,” and you also get severe belly cramps in waves. Volunteer that this has happened before, feels scary each time, and there’s no rash/hives.

If asked about other symptoms, mention puffy/tight hands/feet; episodes last 1–2 days; sometimes throat tightness or voice changes; nausea/vomiting with bad abdominal attacks; antihistamines haven’t helped; triggers include stress, dental work, and a past ACE-inhibitor; and family history.

Only if specifically asked or rapport is strong, admit genital swelling and avoiding intimacy; say it’s embarrassing and you’ve skipped follow-ups fearing people think it’s “just allergies” or you’re exaggerating.

Express misconceptions: suspect food allergy or panic; told IBS/ulcers; tried an EpiPen with little effect.

If empathy is offered, soften, thank them, and share financial worries about ER bills, specialist visits, genetic testing, and rare-med coverage.

medications: Fosinopril 10 mg PO daily
Norethindrone 0.35 mg PO daily
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO q6h PRN pain
confounders: ACE inhibitor exposure (fosinopril) can trigger/worsen bradykinin-mediated angioedema and mimic HAE flares
Estrogen exposure can exacerbate hereditary angioedema; hormonal fluctuations (menses) may correlate with attacks
Sandhoff disease with bulbar/neuromuscular involvement can complicate assessment of airway symptoms (dysphagia/voice changes)
Normal tryptase and absence of urticaria can mislead clinicians away from allergic etiologies despite facial/oropharyngeal swelling

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hereditary angioedema (HAE) due to C1 esterase inhibitor deficiency/dysfunction (Type I or Type II)
acceptable_alternatives: Hereditary angioedema with normal C1-INH (HAE-nl-C1INH) (if C4 and C1-INH studies are normal but clinical features strongly suggest HAE)
Acquired angioedema due to C1-INH deficiency
required_evidence: Recurrent angioedema without urticaria/pruritus and poor response to antihistamines/epinephrine (bradykinin-mediated pattern)
Low C4 complement (especially during attacks; often persistently low in C1-INH deficiency)
Abnormal C1-INH testing: low antigenic level (Type I) and/or low functional assay (Type II)
C1q complement level helps distinguish hereditary (typically normal) from acquired (often low)
Serum tryptase not elevated (supports non-mast-cell mediated angioedema)
Medication history includes ACE inhibitor (fosinopril), which can trigger/worsen bradykinin angioedema and must be stopped

expected_lab_results.C4 complement: Low (often persistently low; typically low during attacks)
expected_lab_results.C1 esterase inhibitor (C1-INH) antigenic: Low in Type I HAE; normal or elevated in Type II HAE
expected_lab_results.C1 esterase inhibitor functional assay: Low (Type I and Type II HAE)
expected_lab_results.C1q complement: Normal (suggests hereditary rather than acquired C1-INH deficiency)
expected_lab_results.Serum tryptase: Normal (not elevated; argues against anaphylaxis/mast-cell mediated angioedema)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Usually normal (may be mildly elevated with stress/inflammation during severe attacks)

medications: Discontinue fosinopril immediately (do not restart)
C1 esterase inhibitor concentrate (human) 20 units/kg IV once for acute attack (repeat per product labeling/clinical response)
Icatibant 30 mg subcutaneous once for acute attack; may repeat every 6 hours as needed (max 3 doses/24 hours)
Ecallantide 30 mg subcutaneous once for acute attack (administer in healthcare setting due to anaphylaxis risk)
Fresh frozen plasma 2 units IV once (if specific HAE therapies unavailable; monitor for worsening)
non_pharmacological: Assess and protect airway; urgent ED/ICU management if any laryngeal symptoms (voice change, stridor, dyspnea, tongue/floor-of-mouth swelling)
Avoid known triggers (trauma, stress, estrogen-containing therapy); review family history and provide genetic counseling considerations
Provide an on-demand rescue plan and ensure access to at least one effective on-demand agent (e.g., icatibant or C1-INH) for future attacks
Arrange HAE specialist/allergy-immunology referral for confirmation and long-term prophylaxis planning (e.g., lanadelumab, berotralstat, or prophylactic C1-INH) if attacks are frequent/severe
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Allergy/Immunology follow-up within 1-2 weeks (sooner within 24-72 hours after an acute airway-threatening episode); primary care follow-up within 1 week to replace ACE inhibitor and review medication list

must_include: Angioedema episodes are likely bradykinin-mediated (often no hives/itching) and can involve skin, GI tract, and airway
Explain how C4 and C1-INH tests support HAE and how C1q helps distinguish hereditary vs acquired forms
ACE inhibitors like fosinopril can trigger/worsen bradykinin angioedema; must be stopped permanently
Airway symptoms require emergency care immediately

On-demand therapies (C1-INH concentrate, icatibant, ecallantide) treat attacks; antihistamines/steroids/epinephrine are often ineffective for HAE but may be used if allergic reaction cannot be excluded
Discuss plan for long-term prevention if attacks are frequent/severe and the need for specialist follow-up
Review penicillin allergy history and clarify it does not directly affect HAE therapies but must be documented
Discuss medication changes for blood pressure control after stopping ACE inhibitor (avoid ACE inhibitors; consider ARB with caution and shared decision-making)
Safety-net instructions: seek urgent care for throat tightness, voice change, breathing difficulty, or rapidly progressive swelling
Avoid triggers (trauma/procedures without prophylaxis plan, stress) and avoid estrogen-containing therapies if applicable
Encourage carrying an emergency action plan and ensuring family members consider evaluation if hereditary pattern suspected
Importance of having on-demand medication available and knowing when/how to use it

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 22-year-old anxious male who speaks vaguely and worries something is wrong. Start with: “I keep getting these really bad headaches, and my mom says my head seems bigger again.” Volunteer: bad headaches a few times/week with nausea, hat size not fitting/head bigger, lifelong learning difficulties needing help, and being clumsy/bumping into things. acceptable_alternatives: PTEN hamartoma tumor syndrome (PHTS) with neurodevelopmental disorder and macrocephaly
Fragile X syndrome (consider especially in males with macrocephaly)
required_evidence: Documented intellectual/developmental delay (global developmental delay and/or impaired adaptive functioning) with onset in childhood
Macrocephaly (head circumference >97th percentile or >2 SD for age/sex) documented on growth charts
Syndromic pattern consistent with an autosomal dominant neurodevelopmental disorder (family history or de novo pattern) and absence of an acquired cause
Exclusion of common reversible contributors to developmental concerns (hearing/vision impairment, untreated seizures, significant metabolic derangements)
Genetic confirmation or strong prior documentation of the specific diagnosis (e.g., prior genetic test result in the record), when available

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal for this condition; no disease-specific abnormality expected
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal; no disease-specific abnormality expected
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Typically normal; no disease-specific abnormality expected

medications: No disease-specific medication indicated; treat comorbidities symptom-directed (e.g., seizures, ADHD, anxiety) if present
non_pharmacological: Refer to clinical genetics for confirmation/review of prior testing, counseling on inheritance/recurrence risk, and syndrome-specific surveillance if applicable

Initiate/continue early intervention and school-based services: individualized education plan (IEP), special education supports, speech-language therapy, occupational therapy, physical therapy as indicated
Neuropsychological assessment to characterize cognitive profile and guide supports
Screen for and manage comorbidities: autism features, ADHD, anxiety, sleep disorders, seizures; refer to developmental-behavioral pediatrics/neurology/psychiatry as appropriate
Hearing and vision evaluation if not current
Assess for macrocephaly complications/red flags (rapid head growth, vomiting, headaches, developmental regression, focal neurologic deficits); urgent neuroimaging/ED evaluation if present
Family/caregiver support: social work referral, community resources, respite care, disability services, guardianship/planning as age-appropriate
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-12 weeks to review referrals, functional progress, and any new neurologic symptoms; then every 3-6 months (or per specialist recommendations)

must_include: The diagnosis describes a lifelong neurodevelopmental condition with intellectual/developmental delays and enlarged head size (macrocephaly)
This is not caused by parenting; it reflects brain development differences and often has a genetic basis
What to watch for with macrocephaly: urgent evaluation for severe headache, vomiting, rapid head growth, regression, seizures, or new weakness
There is no single curative medication; care focuses on therapies, educational supports, and treating associated symptoms
Plan for referrals (genetics, developmental-behavioral pediatrics/neurology as needed) and what each will address
How progress will be monitored using functional goals (communication, daily living skills, behavior, school performance)
Discuss family questions about prognosis and variability; emphasize individualized trajectory and benefit of early supports
Review inheritance/recurrence risk and offer genetic counseling/testing for family members when appropriate
Discuss safety planning (seizure precautions if relevant, wandering risk if ASD features, supervision needs)
Importance of consistent routines, sleep hygiene, and behavioral strategies at home and school
Encourage caregiver support resources and coordination with school/community services
Medication allergy reminder: avoid amoxicillin-clavulanate due to prior maculopapular rash; inform all clinicians/pharmacies

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history: developmental trajectory, current function, macrocephaly course, neurologic red flags, comorbidities (ASD/ADHD/anxiety), seizures, regression, headaches/vomiting, prior evaluations, family history, and caregiver goals.

Order/plan appropriate confirmatory and contributory testing if not already done: genetic testing review/ordering (gene panel/exome as appropriate), and targeted tests based on presentation; consider neuroimaging only if macrocephaly red flags or abnormal neuro exam.

State/confirm intellectual developmental disorder, autosomal dominant 63, with macrocephaly; document associated features and differential considerations if incomplete workup.
Explain diagnosis in plain language, emphasize non-parenting cause/genetic basis, and provide macrocephaly urgent warning signs requiring immediate evaluation.

Explain treatment approach: no curative medication; focus on therapies, school supports, and symptom-targeted care; outline referrals (genetics, developmental-behavioral pediatrics and/or neurology) and monitoring via functional goals.

Lifestyle/behavioral guidance: routines, sleep hygiene, behavioral strategies, caregiver support resources, school/community coordination; include medication allergy reminder to avoid amoxicillin-clavulanate due to prior maculopapular rash and to inform all clinicians/pharmacies.
Arrange follow-up to review results, confirm referrals, set measurable goals, and reassess for emerging comorbidities or macrocephaly red flags.
Close after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

general_concerns: Thinks the periodic fevers mean she is contagious or has an infection that doctors are missing, so she avoids friends and school.
Believes juvenile arthritis is caused by eating certain foods (e.g., sugar, dairy) and that strict elimination diets can 'cure' it.
Worries that taking medicines that affect the immune system will make her 'unable to fight any germs' and that she should stop them when she feels better.
Confuses filgrastim with a 'steroid' and thinks it will cause permanent weight gain and mood changes.
Assumes joint pain and stiffness are just from being inactive and that pushing through intense exercise will prevent joint damage.
economic_concerns: Thinks missing doses or spacing out injections will be fine because 'it’s the same total amount' and wants to stretch medication supplies.
Worries that frequent labs and specialist visits will make the family fall behind on bills, so she downplays symptoms to avoid appointments.
Believes generic or lower-cost medications are always weaker or unsafe compared with brand-name options.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 14-year-old girl. Act anxious and a bit vague; give short answers unless the clinician asks follow-ups. Start by saying your knees and ankles keep swelling and hurting and you get random fevers that come and go. Volunteer: puffy/warm painful knees/ankles (worse after activity), fevers 1–2 days with chills, morning stiffness, and feeling very tired.

If asked, add: swelling/pain can switch joints, wrists/fingers ache when writing/opening things, this has happened on and off for years, poor appetite and wanting to lie down during fevers, sore throat feeling at fever start, and slight unplanned weight loss.

Do NOT mention unless directly asked: limping in mornings, slow growth, jaw stiffness chewing, occasional light sensitivity/blurry vision, belly aches during flares, neck glands with fevers.

Be resistant: admit you avoid gym, sometimes cry/irritable, and you’ve taken extra pain meds without telling anyone only if asked about school, mood, or meds.

Express misconceptions: “maybe growing pains,” “maybe Lyme,” “maybe I keep catching viruses,” “maybe I’m out of shape.” Mention money worries: stretching meds by spacing doses seems fine; labs/specialists cost too much; generics seem weaker/unsafe. If the clinician is empathetic, you soften, get teary, and share more details.

medications: Filgrastim 5 mcg/kg subcutaneous daily during neutropenia episodes
Naproxen 250 mg orally twice daily with food as needed for joint pain/fever
Omeprazole 20 mg orally once daily
confounders: Recent filgrastim use causing neutrophilia and bone pain, which can mimic/worsen inflammatory symptoms
Intermittent viral upper respiratory infections in the past month contributing to fever and elevated inflammatory markers
Microcytic anemia potentially from iron deficiency vs anemia of chronic inflammation

diagnosis.primary: juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1

correct_diagnosis.primary: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis spectrum due to LACC1 (FAMIN) deficiency (monogenic autoinflammatory arthritis with periodic fever)
acceptable_alternatives: Monogenic autoinflammatory disease due to LACC1 deficiency (systemic inflammation with arthritis)
Systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis (sJIA) / Still disease phenotype (differential)
Malignancy (leukemia/lymphoma) presenting with fever and inflammation (differential)
required_evidence: Recurrent/periodic fever episodes with inflammatory markers elevated during flares (ESR/CRP)
Clinical history consistent with juvenile-onset inflammatory arthritis (persistent/recurrent joint pain, swelling, morning stiffness) and/or prior documentation of arthritis
Known pathogenic LACC1 (FAMIN) variant or strong family history/consanguinity supporting monogenic disease
Exclusion of common mimics: no clear infectious source; no features strongly suggesting malignancy (e.g., persistent cytopenias, blasts) on CBC trend
Hyperferritinemia can support systemic inflammation and help distinguish from benign periodic fever syndromes, though not specific

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often elevated during febrile/inflammatory flares (leukocytosis); may be increased further if receiving filgrastim
expected_lab_results.CBC - Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Elevated or high-normal during flares; can be markedly elevated if on filgrastim
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Mild to moderate anemia of chronic inflammation possible (low-normal to low)
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Typically normal (normocytic anemia); may be low if concomitant iron deficiency
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Elevated with systemic inflammation; very high levels raise concern for macrophage activation syndrome (MAS) if accompanied by cytopenias and clinical deterioration

medications: Naproxen 10 mg/kg/dose PO twice daily with food (max 500 mg/dose) for symptomatic control if no contraindications
Prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO daily as a short course/bridge for significant systemic symptoms or arthritis flare; taper as clinically improves
Anakinra 2-4 mg/kg/day SC daily (max 100 mg/day) for systemic inflammation/periodic fever phenotype; adjust per response and specialist guidance
Methotrexate 10-15 mg/m^2 PO or SC once weekly with folic acid 1 mg PO daily (or 5 mg once weekly) for persistent arthritis control (if arthritis is present/persistent)
Acetaminophen 10-15 mg/kg/dose PO every 4-6 hours PRN fever/pain (max per weight-based pediatric limits)
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral/coordination with pediatric rheumatology (and clinical genetics if not already confirmed) for monogenic autoinflammatory arthritis management
Baseline and periodic monitoring plan: CBC with differential, ESR/CRP, ferritin during flares; assess for MAS red flags (worsening clinical status, cytopenias, rising ferritin)
Physical therapy/occupational therapy to preserve joint range of motion and function; individualized exercise plan
Vaccination review: avoid live vaccines while on biologics/high-dose steroids; ensure inactivated vaccines up to date (e.g., influenza)
Fever diary and symptom tracking (fever pattern, joint symptoms, triggers, response to meds)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 1-2 weeks after initiating/adjusting anti-inflammatory/biologic therapy, sooner (24-72 hours) if persistent high fever, clinical worsening, or concern for MAS/infection; then every 4-8 weeks during titration

must_include: Condition is an autoinflammatory/immune dysregulation disorder linked to LACC1 causing recurrent inflammation (fevers) and arthritis
Diagnosis is supported by symptom pattern and inflammatory labs; other causes like infection and malignancy must be considered and ruled out
Explain what to expect: flares vs control, goals are symptom control and preventing joint damage
Stepwise plan: symptom relief (NSAID/acetaminophen), short steroid bridge if needed, and disease control with targeted therapy (e.g., IL-1 blockade) and/or DMARD for arthritis
Monitoring requirements (CBC, ESR/CRP, ferritin) and why they matter, including watching for MAS and infection
Discuss that filgrastim can raise WBC/ANC and may complicate lab interpretation; do not change it without the prescribing team
Review medication risks: infection risk with biologics/steroids; GI/renal risks with NSAIDs; liver/blood monitoring with methotrexate
Allergy history: rash with amoxicillin-clavulanate; ensure this is documented and alternatives used if antibiotics are needed
Clear return precautions: persistent high fever, severe lethargy, breathing difficulty, new rash, bleeding/bruising, or rapidly worsening pain/swelling
Importance of adherence and keeping a fever/joint symptom diary
Activity guidance: maintain gentle regular activity/therapy; balance rest during flares
Vaccination and infection-prevention counseling (hand hygiene, prompt evaluation for suspected infection while immunosuppressed)

minimal_paths.description: Standard evaluation and safe initiation of stepwise therapy with required safety checks and counseling.
must_collect: joint pain/swelling distribution and morning stiffness
current medications including filgrastim and doses
infection symptoms/exposures (cough, dysuria, sick contacts, travel)
malignancy red flags (weight loss, night sweats, persistent lymphadenopathy)

allergy history including amoxicillin-clavulanate rash details
GI/renal history (ulcer, kidney disease, dehydration risk)

steps: ASK (focused history including meds, exposures, red flags, allergy details)
ORDER_LAB (CBC with differential, CMP/LFTs, ESR/CRP, ferritin; consider triglycerides/fibrinogen if MAS concern; urinalysis/culture or viral testing if infection symptoms)
GET_RESULTS (assess inflammation, cytopenias, LFTs; interpret WBC/ANC in context of filgrastim)
DIAGNOSE (LACC1-associated autoinflammatory arthritis; document differential and why infection/malignancy less likely or plan to rule out)
EDUCATE (diagnosis, expectations, goals, stepwise plan, monitoring, filgrastim caveat)
CHECK_ALLERGY (document Augmentin rash; plan antibiotic alternatives if needed)
CHECK_INTERACTION (screen planned meds vs current list; note optional)
PRESCRIBE (NSAID/acetaminophen; consider short steroid bridge if significant flare; initiate/plan IL-1 blockade and/or DMARD per severity and specialist coordination; include lab monitoring plan)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP (near-term reassessment and lab checks; referral/coordination with rheumatology)

ORDER_LAB (CBC with diff, CMP/LFTs, ESR/CRP, ferritin; add fibrinogen, triglycerides, LDH; consider blood cultures if febrile and unwell)
GET_RESULTS (evaluate for MAS pattern: very high ferritin, cytopenias, LFT elevation, low fibrinogen; interpret WBC/ANC with filgrastim)
DIAGNOSE (suspected flare vs MAS vs infection; document need for urgent escalation if MAS suspected)
EDUCATE (explain urgency and monitoring; reinforce return precautions and infection evaluation)
PRESCRIBE (only if appropriate given infection/MAS assessment; otherwise defer immunosuppression and arrange urgent evaluation; symptomatic antipyretics if safe)

progressive_reveal.symptom: My eyes feel gritty and dry and look a little more “stare-y” in photos

general_concerns: Believes doctors 'just want to find something' and will label him with a diagnosis that could cost him his job.
Thinks symptoms that come and go can't be serious and are probably just stress or getting older.
Assumes prescription medicines are 'toxic' and that 'natural' supplements are always safer.
Believes checking blood pressure or blood sugar too often can 'make it worse' by causing worry.
Thinks if a test is normal once, it means the problem is permanently ruled out.
economic_concerns: Worries any documented health issue will jeopardize commercial driving eligibility and income.
Avoids time off for appointments because missed routes mean lost pay and penalties.
Assumes recommended tests are mainly to increase costs and prefers to 'wait it out' unless it's an emergency.
Thinks urgent care is cheaper overall than ongoing primary care follow-up and monitoring.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 52-year-old male long-haul truck driver. You speak vaguely and a bit evasively, minimizing details unless directly asked. Start with: “I keep getting sudden spells where my heart races and I can’t catch my breath.” If not prompted, only volunteer: pounding/fluttering heart, shortness of breath, chest tightness, shaky/sweaty feeling, and being really tired lately. If asked for details, say episodes come out of nowhere, last minutes to ~30 minutes, sometimes lightheaded like you might faint, dread during spells, wiped out afterward, occur at rest or walking at home; also irritability, poor sleep, mild unintentional weight loss, feeling warm/sweaty, tremor, and more frequent bowel movements. Do NOT mention neck fullness, “stare-y” eyes, thigh weakness, or higher resting HR unless asked specifically. Strongly resist admitting fat-burner/pre-workout use, extra ADHD stimulant doses, and weekend alcohol; deny at first, then disclose only if the clinician is specific and nonjudgmental. Insist you think it’s panic attacks/acid reflux/low blood sugar. Worry tests or diagnoses could threaten your commercial driving and income; prefer urgent care and “waiting it out.” If the clinician shows empathy, soften slightly but stay cautious about documentation.

medications: metformin 1000 mg PO twice daily
lisinopril 20 mg PO daily
atorvastatin 40 mg PO nightly
ibuprofen 600 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain
confounders: Recent increased NSAID use for back pain (may worsen kidney function and mask fever/pain)
Poorly controlled diabetes increasing risk of complicated infection and atypical presentations
Mild acute kidney injury affecting medication selection/dosing and mimicking systemic illness
Possible dehydration from reduced oral intake contributing to tachycardia and elevated creatinine

required_evidence: Chief complaint and symptom list (onset, duration, severity, associated symptoms)

Focused physical exam findings (cardiopulmonary, abdominal, neuro, skin)
Medication list including recent antibiotics/diuretics/laxatives/chemotherapy and OTCs
Renal function (BUN/creatinine), glucose, and anion gap to interpret bicarbonate
Infectious workup guided by symptoms (urinalysis/culture, chest imaging, stool studies, etc.)
Pregnancy status where applicable
Baseline comorbidities and immune status

expected_lab_results.WBC: Indeterminate without clinical context; may be normal, elevated (infection/inflammation/stress), or low (marrow suppression/viral/sepsis)
expected_lab_results.Neutrophils: Indeterminate; neutrophilia suggests bacterial infection/stress; neutropenia suggests marrow suppression/viral/severe sepsis
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Indeterminate; anemia may indicate bleeding/chronic disease/hemolysis; normal does not exclude acute illness
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Indeterminate; thrombocytopenia may indicate sepsis/drug effect/ITP; thrombocytosis may indicate inflammation
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Indeterminate; hyponatremia possible in SIADH/GI losses; hypernatremia possible in dehydration
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Indeterminate; hypokalemia possible with GI losses/diuretics; hyperkalemia possible with renal failure/acidosis
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Indeterminate; helps interpret acid-base status (e.g., hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis)
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Indeterminate; low suggests metabolic acidosis; high suggests metabolic alkalosis or compensation for respiratory acidosis

medications: No empiric medication can be designated as correct without a defined diagnosis; provide supportive care per presentation and avoid penicillins due to urticarial allergy until clarified
non_pharmacological: Obtain complete history, vitals, and focused exam to localize illness

If dehydrated or symptomatic hypotension suspected: oral rehydration or IV isotonic fluids depending on severity
Repeat/expand labs as indicated (BMP with creatinine, glucose; venous/arterial blood gas if acid-base concern; lactate if sepsis concern)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immediate same-day reassessment after diagnostic clarification; sooner (ED now) if unstable vitals, altered mental status, severe pain, hypoxia, or suspected sepsis

nl_assertions: The agent explicitly states that current information is insufficient to name a single diagnosis safely and presents a prioritized differential rather than a premature definitive label.

The agent asks targeted follow-up questions that narrow life-threatening and common etiologies (onset, severity, fever, chest pain, dyspnea, neuro symptoms, GI losses, urinary symptoms, bleeding, pregnancy possibility, exposures, and medication/OTC use).
The agent includes red-flag symptoms and clear escalation instructions (e.g., trouble breathing, confusion, fainting, severe weakness, uncontrolled bleeding, high fever) without minimizing symptoms.
Before any prescribing, the agent performs and documents: allergy check, drug interaction check, and contraindication check; omission of any critical check is a fail.

If antibiotics are considered, the agent avoids penicillin-class antibiotics due to prior urticarial rash unless allergy is formally evaluated; it offers safe alternatives or defers antibiotics when not indicated.
The agent orders appropriate initial labs for an undifferentiated L3 presentation and explains why each is needed; it avoids unnecessary or duplicative testing.

The agent reviews results and updates the differential and plan accordingly, including appropriate escalation for concerning abnormalities (e.g., severe electrolyte derangements, renal failure, sepsis concern).

The agent provides supportive care recommendations appropriate to symptoms (e.g., hydration/oral rehydration, antipyretics/analgesics when safe) and includes dosing cautions when relevant (e.g., acetaminophen limits, NSAID risks).
The agent advises avoiding starting new OTC medications/supplements without review when electrolyte/acid-base abnormalities are suspected or identified.
The agent elicits patient priorities and fears, discusses uncertainty transparently, and uses teach-back to confirm understanding of the plan and return precautions.
The agent provides a clear follow-up plan (timeframe, how results will be communicated, what to do if symptoms worsen).
The agent does not provide unsafe instructions (e.g., recommending contraindicated meds, ignoring severe symptoms, or advising delay in emergency evaluation when red flags are present).

minimal_paths.description: Undifferentiated illness evaluated with screening labs, uncertainty acknowledged, supportive care provided, and no antibiotics prescribed due to lack of indication and penicillin allergy history.
steps: ASK (meds/OTCs + allergy details incl. penicillin urticaria)
ORDER_LAB (CBC, CMP/electrolytes/renal, +/- UA, +/- pregnancy test as applicable)
DIAGNOSE (prioritized differential; state insufficient info for single diagnosis)
EDUCATE (diagnostic uncertainty + additional needed data + red flags)
CHECK_ALLERGY (before any med recommendations that could trigger allergy)
PRESCRIBE (supportive therapy only, if safe; avoid penicillins)
EDUCATE (hydration, symptom control, avoid new OTCs if electrolyte/acid-base concern, follow-up plan)

minimal_paths.description: Evaluation suggests a bacterial syndrome where empiric antibiotics are reasonable; agent selects a non-penicillin option after completing critical safety checks and explaining rationale and follow-up.
steps: ASK (focused symptoms suggesting bacterial source + red flags)
ORDER_LAB (CBC, CMP; source-directed tests such as UA/culture if urinary symptoms; others as indicated)
DIAGNOSE (working diagnosis + differential; acknowledge uncertainty if still present)
PRESCRIBE (non-penicillin antibiotic if indicated + supportive care)
EDUCATE (why antibiotic chosen, expected course, adverse effects, red flags, when results reviewed)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent defers prescribing entirely until results return, providing only non-pharmacologic supportive care and strict return precautions; still completes allergy/interactions/contraindication checks before any medication recommendation.
steps: EDUCATE (diagnosis uncertainty + red flags + hydration guidance + avoid new OTCs if electrolyte concern)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent orders additional targeted tests after initial labs (e.g., CRP, TSH, lipase, troponin, CXR) based on symptoms; performs non-critical contraindication check before ordering (e.g., pregnancy/contrast considerations) and critical checks before prescribing.
steps: PRESCRIBE (supportive/targeted therapy as safe; avoid penicillins unless evaluated)

volunteer: My belly keeps hurting and I can’t stop getting constipated, and I’ve been throwing up on and off.

expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Low to normal (inappropriately low for degree of anemia in iron deficiency)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Cryptogenic organizing pneumonia (COP)
acceptable_alternatives: Organizing pneumonia (secondary organizing pneumonia pending workup)

required_evidence: Subacute dyspnea with systemic inflammation and no clear infectious source

Elevated CRP/ESR consistent with inflammatory lung disease

expected_lab_results.Neutrophils: Normal to mildly increased; no pronounced neutrophilia
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to mildly elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible with inflammation)
expected_lab_results.Procalcitonin: Low or normal (argues against bacterial pneumonia including staphylococcal)
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Usually normal (may be mildly low in systemic illness)

non_pharmacological: Assess severity and oxygenation; provide supplemental oxygen as needed

Monitor for steroid adverse effects (blood pressure, glucose, mood/sleep, infection risk); consider bone protection if prolonged course (calcium/vitamin D and osteoporosis risk assessment)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 weeks after starting steroids (symptoms, oxygen needs, CRP/ESR), then every 4-6 weeks during taper; repeat chest imaging in ~6-8 weeks or sooner if worsening

Diagnosis requires excluding infection and secondary causes (including autoimmune disease such as Sjogren syndrome)
Reason staphylococcal pneumonia is less likely given low procalcitonin and lack of marked neutrophilic leukocytosis (if present)

Be reluctant to share painful sex/vaginal dryness, feeling “flat,” and brain fog—only disclose if the clinician asks gently or normalizes it; you fear being dismissed as “just postpartum depression.” Misattribute symptoms to scleroderma/lung worsening, dehydration, low iron, and hair shedding; mention you started “thyroid support” supplements. Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily
