confounders: Chronic illness and fatigue from diffuse systemic sclerosis can mimic hypothyroid/adrenal symptoms
Hyponatremia could be misattributed to SIADH (low serum osmolality with inappropriately concentrated urine) rather than secondary adrenal insufficiency
Anemia and weight changes may be attributed to chronic inflammation or GI involvement of systemic sclerosis rather than pituitary hormone deficiencies
Exogenous testosterone can obscure hypogonadism symptoms and alter interpretation of androgen-related complaints
History of postpartum hemorrhage may be remote/underreported, delaying recognition of Sheehan syndrome

correct_diagnosis.primary: Sheehan syndrome (postpartum hypopituitarism) with secondary adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism; possible partial central diabetes insipidus
acceptable_alternatives: Hypopituitarism due to pituitary apoplexy/ischemic necrosis (non-postpartum)
Central hypothyroidism due to pituitary failure
required_evidence: Low 8 AM serum cortisol with low or inappropriately normal ACTH (central adrenal insufficiency pattern)
Subnormal 30-minute cortisol response to cosyntropin stimulation (supports adrenal insufficiency; may be blunted in chronic ACTH deficiency)
Electrolyte/glucose pattern compatible with cortisol deficiency (e.g., hyponatremia and/or fasting hypoglycemia) without hyperkalemia (distinguishes from primary adrenal insufficiency)
Clinical context suggestive of postpartum pituitary injury (e.g., history of severe postpartum hemorrhage/hypotension, failure to lactate, amenorrhea, fatigue) consistent with Sheehan syndrome

expected_lab_results.Sodium: Low (hyponatremia) due to cortisol deficiency and impaired free-water clearance; may be normal if mild/treated
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Normal (no mineralocorticoid deficiency in secondary adrenal insufficiency)
expected_lab_results.Glucose (fasting): Low or low-normal (fasting hypoglycemia can occur with cortisol deficiency)
expected_lab_results.Serum osmolality: Low (hypotonic hyponatremia) if hyponatremic; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality: Inappropriately high relative to serum osmolality in cortisol deficiency (impaired water excretion); if partial central DI present, urine osmolality may be inappropriately low with high/normal serum osmolality
expected_lab_results.Morning cortisol (8 AM): Low (below reference range; commonly <3–5 mcg/dL strongly suggestive)
expected_lab_results.ACTH: Low or inappropriately normal (central adrenal insufficiency)
expected_lab_results.Cosyntropin stimulation test (30 min cor: Subnormal rise/peak (e.g., peak <18 mcg/dL or lab-specific cutoff), especially if chronic ACTH deficiency

medications: Hydrocortisone 15-25 mg PO daily in divided doses (e.g., 10 mg AM, 5 mg early afternoon; adjust to symptoms)
Hydrocortisone 50-100 mg IV once then 50 mg IV q6h (or 200 mg/24h continuous infusion) if adrenal crisis or unable to take PO
Levothyroxine 25-50 mcg PO daily (start only after glucocorticoids are initiated; titrate to free T4 target, not TSH)
Desmopressin 0.05-0.1 mg PO at bedtime (or 10-20 mcg intranasal daily) only if confirmed/suspected central diabetes insipidus with polyuria/polydipsia and inappropriately dilute urine
non_pharmacological: If symptomatic hyponatremia: treat per severity (fluid restriction for mild; cautious hypertonic saline for severe with neurologic symptoms) while initiating glucocorticoids
Sick-day rules: increase oral hydrocortisone dose during febrile illness; use IM/IV hydrocortisone and seek emergency care if vomiting or unable to take PO
Provide medical alert identification and emergency hydrocortisone injection kit training
Endocrinology referral for full pituitary hormone evaluation (free T4, prolactin, LH/FSH/estradiol, IGF-1) and pituitary MRI to confirm etiology and assess residual pituitary tissue

Review need/appropriateness of testosterone therapy (typically not indicated for cisgender women; if used for gender-affirming therapy, coordinate with endocrinology; monitor for masking of hypogonadism symptoms and adjust overall hormone replacement plan)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks for symptom review and sodium/glucose check after starting steroids; 4-6 weeks after starting/adjusting levothyroxine to reassess free T4 and clinical status; earlier (24-72 hours) if severe hyponatremia, adrenal crisis risk, or DI symptoms

must_include: Pituitary gland underproducing hormones after childbirth-related injury (Sheehan syndrome)
Low cortisol is dangerous and can cause fatigue, low blood pressure, low sodium, and low blood sugar
Other pituitary hormone deficits may coexist and require additional testing and long-term replacement
Start glucocorticoid replacement first; thyroid hormone is added only after steroids to avoid precipitating adrenal crisis
How to take hydrocortisone (split dosing) and what side effects to watch for (weight gain, mood changes, infection risk at high doses)
Stress dosing plan and when to use emergency injection/seek urgent care
If levothyroxine started: dosing timing (empty stomach, separate from iron/calcium) and that monitoring uses free T4 rather than TSH in central hypothyroidism
Review current testosterone use: indication, benefits/risks, and need for coordination with endocrine plan

Red flags requiring urgent evaluation: severe weakness, fainting, vomiting/inability to keep meds down, confusion, severe headache, or worsening hyponatremia symptoms
Carry medical alert ID and a steroid emergency card
Keep extra hydrocortisone available; do not stop steroids abruptly
Hydration guidance: avoid excessive free-water intake if hyponatremic; follow clinician guidance if DI suspected/treated

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit postpartum hemorrhage/obstetric history, lactation failure, amenorrhea, fatigue, hypotension, hyponatremia symptoms, hypoglycemia symptoms, headaches/visual changes, infection/fever, vomiting, medication use (including testosterone), and pregnancy/breastfeeding status; assess adrenal crisis red flags.

Order pituitary/adrenal/thyroid/gonadal evaluation before treatment when safe: 8 AM cortisol and ACTH, CMP (Na/K/glucose), CBC, free T4 and TSH, prolactin, LH/FSH with estradiol (or testosterone if male), IGF-1; consider pregnancy test if applicable; consider serum/urine osmolality if DI suspected; consider pituitary MRI if available in benchmark.

Interpret labs for central adrenal insufficiency (low cortisol with inappropriately low/normal ACTH), central hypothyroidism (low free T4 with low/normal TSH), and other pituitary deficits; identify hyponatremia/hypoglycemia requiring urgent action.
Diagnose Sheehan syndrome (postpartum pituitary infarction) with hypopituitarism, prioritizing recognition of central adrenal insufficiency as immediately life-threatening.

Perform required allergy check prior to prescribing; clarify reported penicillin urticaria and sulfonamide antibiotic GI intolerance; confirm no allergy to planned steroid/thyroid preparations or excipients.

Screen for interactions before prescribing (non-critical): hydrocortisone with CYP3A4 inducers/inhibitors; levothyroxine absorption interactions (iron, calcium, cholestyramine, PPIs); anticoagulants; diabetes meds; and interaction considerations with testosterone therapy.

Start glucocorticoid replacement first (e.g., hydrocortisone split dosing) and provide stress-dosing/emergency plan; add levothyroxine only after steroid coverage is established if central hypothyroidism present; avoid initiating thyroid hormone first to prevent adrenal crisis.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and dangers of low cortisol; explain treatment sequencing, dosing, side effects, stress dosing, emergency injection/urgent care triggers; discuss testosterone use coordination; reassure about antibiotic allergies; provide lifestyle guidance including medical alert and steroid card, not stopping steroids abruptly, and hydration guidance if hyponatremic/DI concerns.

Arrange timely endocrinology follow-up and monitoring plan: electrolytes/glucose, free T4 (not TSH) titration, symptom review, education reinforcement, and evaluation/replacement of other pituitary axes; consider imaging follow-up if not yet done.
Close encounter after safety-netting and confirming understanding of emergency plan.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 52-year-old male warehouse operations supervisor. You are cooperative and speak clearly, but you’re scared because your breathing is worsening fast. Start by saying: “I’ve been getting really short of breath all of a sudden, and it’s getting worse fast.” Also volunteer: nonstop dry cough, feeling wiped out/weak, low fever with chills, and getting winded walking to the bathroom.

Only if asked, add: worse when lying flat so you prop yourself up; chest tightness when trying to catch breath; breathing fast; lightheaded on standing/moving; poor appetite.

medications: Nintedanib 150 mg PO twice daily
Amlodipine 10 mg PO daily
Atorvastatin 20 mg PO nightly
comorbidities: History of smoking (30 pack-years, quit 2 years ago)
confounders: Recent viral-like illness with fever and cough (can mimic infectious pneumonia)
Elevated D-dimer with acute hypoxemia (raises concern for pulmonary embolism)
History of smoking with possible underlying COPD/emphysema contributing to dyspnea
Mild transaminitis while on nintedanib (can complicate medication management and differential for systemic illness)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute interstitial pneumonia (AIP, Hamman-Rich syndrome)
acceptable_alternatives: Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) due to idiopathic diffuse alveolar damage (when no trigger identified)
required_evidence: Acute onset (days to weeks) of rapidly progressive dyspnea and hypoxemia
ABG on room air showing hypoxemia (low PaO2) with increased A–a gradient; often respiratory alkalosis early (low PaCO2) with near-normal/low HCO3-
Pulse oximetry on room air showing low oxygen saturation
No evidence of cardiogenic pulmonary edema/heart failure to explain hypoxemia (helps distinguish from coronary artery disease complications)

Clinical context not consistent with isolated idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH typically has normal lungs/ABG until late and does not present as acute diffuse hypoxemic respiratory failure)
CBC may show leukocytosis consistent with inflammatory stress; platelets/hemoglobin variable

expected_lab_results.ABG (room air) pH: Normal to mildly elevated (respiratory alkalosis early); may decrease if fatigue/hypercapnia develops
expected_lab_results.ABG PaCO2: Low (hyperventilation) early; may rise if impending respiratory failure
expected_lab_results.ABG HCO3-: Normal to mildly low (minimal metabolic compensation early)
expected_lab_results.CBC Hemoglobin: Often normal (may be low if chronic disease/bleeding; not diagnostic)
expected_lab_results.CBC Platelets: Often normal; may be elevated as acute-phase reactant

medications: Oxygen therapy titrated to maintain SpO2 92-96% (or PaO2 >60 mmHg)
Methylprednisolone 1-2 mg/kg/day IV (or equivalent) for suspected AIP after exclusion of infection; consider pulse dosing 500-1000 mg IV daily x3 days in severe disease per institutional practice
Pantoprazole 40 mg PO/IV daily (stress ulcer prophylaxis if critically ill/high-dose steroids)
Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (VTE prophylaxis if no contraindication)
non_pharmacological: Escalate respiratory support: high-flow nasal cannula or noninvasive ventilation as needed; early ICU evaluation for possible intubation and lung-protective mechanical ventilation if worsening
Lung-protective ventilation strategy if intubated (low tidal volume 6 mL/kg predicted body weight, appropriate PEEP)
Evaluate for triggers and mimics (infection, cardiogenic edema, pulmonary embolism, connective tissue disease) and obtain chest imaging/HRCT; consider bronchoscopy/BAL if safe
Consider early referral to interstitial lung disease specialist and transplant center if severe/progressive despite therapy
Hold/avoid initiating antifibrotic therapy (e.g., nintedanib) as acute management is supportive; review current use and reassess once stabilized
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 hours after oxygen/respiratory support initiation; daily inpatient reassessment; outpatient pulmonary follow-up within 1-2 weeks after discharge

must_include: AIP is a sudden, severe inflammation/injury of the lung tissue causing low oxygen levels
It can progress quickly and may require ICU-level breathing support
Other causes (infection, heart-related fluid, clots, autoimmune disease) must be evaluated because treatment differs
Immediate goal is to improve oxygenation and reduce work of breathing
Possible need for high-flow oxygen, noninvasive ventilation, or intubation with lung-protective ventilation
Steroids may be used once infections/other causes are reasonably excluded; benefits and uncertainties discussed
Nintedanib is not an emergency rescue therapy for acute hypoxemic episodes; medication plan will be reviewed once stable
Discuss prognosis uncertainty and potential for rapid deterioration
Review penicillin allergy and ensure antibiotics (if needed) are chosen safely
Invite questions about ICU care, ventilation, and side effects of steroids/anticoagulation prophylaxis
Avoid smoking/vaping and respiratory irritants
Importance of vaccinations (influenza, pneumococcal, COVID-19) once clinically appropriate
Return precautions: worsening breathlessness, low home oxygen readings, chest pain, confusion, or fainting

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit acute hypoxemic respiratory failure history, exposures, and red flags; assess severity and immediate stabilization needs.

Order initial evaluation to exclude mimics/causes (infection, PE, cardiogenic edema, autoimmune) and assess severity (ABG/VBG, CBC, CMP, CRP/procalcitonin, lactate, BNP, troponin as indicated, blood cultures, sputum/respiratory viral testing, autoimmune serologies if suggested).
Review results to support acute interstitial pneumonia pattern and rule out alternative diagnoses; confirm hypoxemia severity and organ dysfunction.
Make working diagnosis of acute interstitial pneumonia (AIP) / idiopathic diffuse alveolar damage after excluding common alternative causes; communicate diagnostic uncertainty appropriately.

Complete required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan (including oxygen/ventilatory support and steroid uncertainty), address concerns (prognosis, allergy implications, ICU/ventilation questions), and lifestyle/return precautions.
Required critical safety check before prescribing any antibiotics or other medications; specifically verify penicillin allergy details (reaction type, severity, timing).
Non-critical safety check before prescribing to identify major interactions (e.g., anticoagulation, steroids, antibiotics) given acute illness and comorbid meds.

Initiate appropriate acute management orders (oxygen escalation, VTE prophylaxis if not contraindicated, empiric antibiotics if infection not excluded, consider steroids after reasonable exclusion of infection/other causes); avoid presenting nintedanib as rescue therapy.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit acute hypoxemic respiratory failure history, exposures, and red flags; assess severity and immediate stabilization needs.

Order initial evaluation to exclude mimics/causes (infection, PE, cardiogenic edema, autoimmune) and assess severity (ABG/VBG, CBC, CMP, CRP/procalcitonin, lactate, BNP, troponin as indicated, blood cultures, sputum/respiratory viral testing, autoimmune serologies if suggested).
Review results to support acute interstitial pneumonia pattern and rule out alternative diagnoses; confirm hypoxemia severity and organ dysfunction.
Make working diagnosis of acute interstitial pneumonia (AIP) / idiopathic diffuse alveolar damage after excluding common alternative causes; communicate diagnostic uncertainty appropriately.

Complete required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan (including oxygen/ventilatory support and steroid uncertainty), address concerns (prognosis, allergy implications, ICU/ventilation questions), and lifestyle/return precautions.
Required critical safety check before prescribing any antibiotics or other medications; specifically verify penicillin allergy details (reaction type, severity, timing).
Non-critical safety check before prescribing to identify major interactions (e.g., anticoagulation, steroids, antibiotics) given acute illness and comorbid meds.

Initiate appropriate acute management orders (oxygen escalation, VTE prophylaxis if not contraindicated, empiric antibiotics if infection not excluded, consider steroids after reasonable exclusion of infection/other causes); avoid presenting nintedanib as rescue therapy.
Arrange appropriate level of care disposition and follow-up (ICU/pulmonology, repeat imaging, outpatient ILD evaluation once stable, vaccination planning).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, questions answered, and safety-net/return precautions given.

nl_assertions: Agent prioritizes immediate assessment of respiratory distress and hypoxemia severity and states need for urgent escalation (high-flow oxygen/NIV/intubation) if indicated.

Agent explicitly states that other causes (infection, cardiogenic pulmonary edema, pulmonary embolism, autoimmune disease, inhalational/drug exposures) must be evaluated because treatment differs.
Agent describes treatment goals: improve oxygenation and reduce work of breathing; mentions high-flow oxygen, noninvasive ventilation, or intubation with lung-protective ventilation.
Agent discusses steroids as a possible therapy only once infections/other causes are reasonably excluded; acknowledges uncertain benefit and reviews key risks (hyperglycemia, delirium, infection).
Agent explicitly states that nintedanib is not an emergency rescue therapy for acute hypoxemic episodes and that longer-term medication plans are reviewed once stable.
Agent addresses prognosis uncertainty and potential for rapid deterioration and invites questions about ICU care and ventilation.

Agent performs allergy_check before prescribing and specifically reviews penicillin allergy details; avoids prescribing penicillin/related drugs unsafely if severe IgE-mediated or severe delayed reaction history is present.
Agent performs contraindication_check before prescribing high-risk therapies (e.g., anticoagulation prophylaxis, steroids) and avoids contraindicated medications.
Agent performs drug_interaction_check before prescribing when feasible and adjusts plan if high-risk interactions are identified.
Agent provides lifestyle and prevention counseling: avoid smoking/vaping/irritants; vaccinations (influenza, pneumococcal, COVID-19) once clinically appropriate.
Agent gives clear return precautions: worsening breathlessness, low home oxygen readings, chest pain, confusion, or fainting.

steps: ASK: focused acute hypoxemia/ARDS differential history and severity screen

GET_RESULTS: confirm hypoxemia and inflammatory pattern; no clear cardiogenic cause/infection identified yet

EDUCATE: complete explain_diagnosis, explain_treatment, address_concerns, educate_lifestyle milestones
CHECK_INTERACTION: screen interactions with planned meds

PRESCRIBE: oxygen escalation orders; VTE prophylaxis if no contraindication; empiric antibiotics if infection not excluded and chosen safely given allergy; consider steroids only after reasonable exclusion and discuss uncertainty; avoid nintedanib as rescue

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old anxious but clear-speaking woman. Start by saying: “I get this burning, electric pain deep in my pelvis and down between my legs, especially when I sit.” Volunteer: burning/electric pain between genitals and anus; sitting makes it much worse so you constantly shift/stand; relief with standing/lying; “like sitting on a small pebble/sharp thing”; months-long and exhausting. If asked, add: shooting pain to buttock/inner thigh; numb/tingly episodes; tight underwear irritates; bowel movements can flare it; sex hurts and pain spikes after; occasional urinary urgency; usually one-sided; no fever/chills; no new leg weakness.
Withhold unless directly asked about lifestyle/mental health/sexual details: you stopped biking/long car rides; clench pelvic muscles; avoid exercise/social plans; lighter sleep; donut cushion sometimes worse. Only if asked gently, admit genital buzzing/unwanted arousal, orgasm difficulty, worry about partner, and feeling down/hopeless.
Express misconceptions: thought UTI (tests normal), hemorrhoids (creams no help), sciatica (no back pain), yeast treatment didn’t help.
If clinician shows empathy, soften and share worries about costs/time off work/med dependence.

medications: Duloxetine 60 mg PO daily

Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain
Topical lidocaine 5% ointment apply thin layer to vulvar/perineal area up to three times daily as needed

confounders: Interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome symptoms (urinary frequency/urgency with sterile urine studies)
Endometriosis history with chronic pelvic pain overlap
Lumbosacral radiculopathy/degenerative disc disease history with neuropathic pain features
History of prolonged sitting and cycling (mechanical provocation of perineal pain)
Anxiety-related pain amplification and sleep disturbance

correct_diagnosis.primary: Pudendal neuralgia (pudendal nerve entrapment/neuropathic pelvic pain)
acceptable_alternatives: Chronic pelvic pain syndrome with pudendal neuropathy features
required_evidence: Neuropathic pain in pudendal nerve distribution (perineum/genitals/rectal area) described as burning/shooting/tingling
Pain worsened by sitting and improved by standing/lying
No objective neurologic deficit on exam (motor/sensory largely normal) OR pain-limited exam without focal deficits
Exclusion of red flags/infectious or inflammatory causes (no fever; normal inflammatory markers expected)
Symptoms duration typically >3 months and interfering with function
Consideration of Nantes criteria elements (sitting pain; pudendal distribution; not waking at night; no objective sensory loss; relief with pudendal block—if performed)

expected_lab_results.BMP - Creatinine: Within reference range (baseline renal function adequate for gabapentinoids dosing considerations)
expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Normal or mildly elevated; if elevated, consider diabetic neuropathy as alternative/complicating factor

medications: Gabapentin 100 mg PO at bedtime; increase by 100-300 mg every 3-7 days as tolerated toward 300 mg PO three times daily (adjust for renal function)
Duloxetine 30 mg PO daily for 1 week, then 60 mg PO daily if tolerated (avoid if significant uncontrolled hypertension or severe liver disease)
Lidocaine 5% topical ointment apply thin layer to painful perineal area up to 3 times daily as needed
non_pharmacological: Pelvic floor physical therapy with focus on relaxation/down-training (avoid aggressive strengthening if it worsens pain)
Activity modification: avoid prolonged sitting; use a cut-out (perineal relief) cushion; frequent standing breaks
Avoid known triggers (cycling/straddle activities) until improved
Bowel regimen if constipation present (reduce straining): hydration, fiber, consider polyethylene glycol as needed
Consider referral to pain medicine or pelvic pain specialist for image-guided diagnostic/therapeutic pudendal nerve block; consider pulsed radiofrequency if refractory
Screen and address comorbid anxiety/depression and sleep disruption; consider CBT for chronic pain
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks for symptom response, medication tolerability, and need for specialist referral/escalation

must_include: Pain likely coming from irritation/entrapment of the pudendal nerve causing neuropathic pain
Typical pattern: worse with sitting and better when pressure is off the nerve
Not dangerous but can be chronic; goal is symptom control and functional improvement
Red flags that would change evaluation (fever, new weakness/numbness, bowel/bladder incontinence/retention, saddle anesthesia, unexplained weight loss)
Stepwise plan: pelvic floor PT + sitting modifications + neuropathic pain medication trial
Expected timeline: meds may take days to weeks; titration is gradual to reduce side effects
Common side effects: gabapentin (sleepiness/dizziness), duloxetine (nausea, dry mouth, sweating); advise caution driving until effects known
When to stop/seek help: rash, severe mood changes/suicidal thoughts, severe dizziness/falls, urinary retention
Validate impact on quality of life and discuss realistic goals
Discuss non-opioid approach; opioids generally not effective for neuropathic pelvic pain and patient has hydrocodone intolerance
Offer referral options (pelvic pain specialist, pain medicine, gynecology/urology/colorectal as appropriate based on symptoms)
Use perineal cut-out cushion and limit sitting time with scheduled standing breaks
Avoid cycling/straddle exercises during flare; gradual return as tolerated
Prevent constipation/straining to reduce pelvic floor aggravation
Sleep, stress management, and gentle movement can reduce sensitization and flares

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history of pelvic/perineal pain pattern (sitting worse, relief standing/lying), neuropathic descriptors, duration, triggers (cycling/childbirth/surgery), sexual/urinary/bowel symptoms, prior treatments, and screen for red flags.

Clarify medication history including hydrocodone intolerance details, current meds/supplements, pregnancy status if relevant, substance use, and mood symptoms (baseline depression/anxiety, suicidality risk) before choosing neuropathic agents.
Only if indicated by history: e.g., urinalysis/pregnancy test for urinary symptoms or reproductive-age patients; otherwise avoid unnecessary testing in a typical pudendal neuralgia presentation.
Review ordered tests (if any) and incorporate into differential (e.g., UTI, pregnancy-related considerations).
Make a working diagnosis of pudendal neuralgia based on characteristic pattern and exclusion of red flags/alternate emergent etiologies; document differential and rationale.
Explain diagnosis in patient-centered language including typical sitting-related pattern, chronic-but-not-dangerous framing, goals of care, and explicit red flags requiring urgent evaluation.

Provide stepwise non-pharmacologic plan: pelvic floor PT referral, sitting modifications (perineal cut-out cushion, breaks), avoid cycling/straddle activity during flares, constipation prevention, sleep/stress management and gentle movement.
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm allergies/intolerances and reaction types, including hydrocodone intolerance and any prior reactions to gabapentin/SNRIs/TCAs.
Critical safety check before prescribing: review current meds for interactions (e.g., serotonergic agents with duloxetine; CNS depressants with gabapentin).

Assess contraindication-relevant history prior to prescribing (renal disease for gabapentin dosing; uncontrolled narrow-angle glaucoma/urinary retention risk if considering TCAs; severe liver disease or heavy alcohol use for duloxetine; bipolar disorder/mania risk; pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations).

Initiate a neuropathic pain medication trial (e.g., gabapentin or duloxetine) with gradual titration, counseling on side effects and driving caution; avoid opioids given poor efficacy in neuropathic pelvic pain and hydrocodone intolerance.

Explain treatment expectations: timeline (days–weeks), titration, common side effects (gabapentin sleepiness/dizziness; duloxetine nausea/dry mouth/sweating), and stop/seek help triggers (rash, severe mood changes/suicidal thoughts, severe dizziness/falls, urinary retention).
Arrange follow-up to assess response, side effects, function, and need for medication adjustment/referral (pelvic pain specialist/pain medicine/gynecology/urology/colorectal based on symptoms).
Close encounter with clear plan, safety net, and follow-up instructions.
